title: A Middle East energy shock tests Sri Lanka’s stabilisation gains
body: Brent crude surged past $116 a barrel—up 52% since 27 February—as disruption at the Strait of Hormuz rippled through fuel and freight markets. The G7 vowed to safeguard energy stability, but the near-term arithmetic for Sri Lanka is unforgiving: higher import costs, pricier logistics, and inflation pass-through. Colombo has moved to triage risk—rationing fuel in pockets, flying in a 38,000MT rescue cargo from India, and securing nearly two months of jet fuel buffer (CAASL)—while Russia signalled willingness to supply energy. Yet electricity tariffs rose again and restaurants lifted prices, while Treasury bill yields edged up (91-day to 7.80%), reflecting tighter financial conditions. The policy trade-off is old but urgent: price pass-through to preserve reserves versus targeted relief to protect the vulnerable. Acronyms explained: G7 is the Group of Seven major economies; CAASL is the Civil Aviation Authority of Sri Lanka. ⚠️🛢️✈️

title: Remittances and migration: Sri Lanka’s biggest macro hedge faces a war-time wobble
body: Roughly half of Sri Lanka’s remittances come from the Gulf; in 2025 about 311,000 people migrated for work, with an average of 862 daily departures—666 to the Middle East. Disruptions to flights and employer activity amid the Iran conflict threaten new deployments, contract renewals, and the timely return of workers after leave. At the household level, a prolonged shock could erase over LKR 150,000 per month in income for affected families; at the macro level, it risks remittance diversion to informal channels, complicating reserve-building. The State’s playbook—reaffirming wage protection with employers, keeping formal channels competitive, and accelerating destination diversification—needs rapid execution. 💸🌍

title: Trincomalee’s oil tanks move from footnote to frontline asset
body: With maritime chokepoints in flux and prices high, the British-era Trincomalee oil tank farm looks like the energy-storage cushion Sri Lanka long promised itself but never built. Agreements exist—initially under the 1987 Indo–Lanka Accord and updated in 2022 with Lanka IOC (Indian Oil’s unit)—but implementation lags. In parallel, analysts flag strategic upsides from converting Trinco into a broader energy hub—storage, refining, bunkering, and green power integration—if governance is clarified and investment de-risked. In a world of supply shocks, storage is strategy. 🛢️⚓

title: Digital rails for inbound rupees: LankaPay links with Weixin Pay and banks harden e‑commerce security
body: To tap tourist spend and diversify FX inflows, LankaPay enabled cross-border QR acceptance for Weixin Pay, letting Chinese visitors scan-and-pay at 400,000+ LANKAQR merchants. In parallel, Commercial Bank became the first to enable 3D Secure (3DS) authentication for LankaPay/JCB cards, bringing national-scheme users up to global e‑commerce security norms. The combination—seamless acceptance plus safer online payments—tilts the payments mix towards traceable, lower-friction inflows. In fintech-speak: better on‑us acquiring, lower chargeback risk, higher conversion. 💳📱

title: Accountability advances—if uneven—as courts bite and ministries digitise
body: The Supreme Court held senior officials—including a former Health Minister—liable for breaching citizens’ fundamental rights in an unlawful medical procurement, ordering personal payments and calling for anti‑corruption action. The Justice Ministry launched a Management Information System to professionalise appointments (e.g., Commissioners of Oaths, Inquirers into Sudden Deaths) and upgraded its website to improve citizen access to mediation, arbitration, and victim-witness services. Yet governance frictions persist: the Energy Minister was indicted over an alleged 2016 corruption offence (he was granted bail), doctors announced a 24‑hour strike over disputed transfers, and the water utility warned of supply cuts if drought worsens. The direction of travel is clearer than the pace. ⚖️🖥️

title: Import substitution that actually substitutes: Sri Lanka’s first SSP plant goes live
body: Lankem commissioned the country’s first Single Super Phosphate (SSP) fertiliser plant (Rs. 3bn), leveraging the long‑discussed Eppawala rock phosphate deposit. After a decade of trials to handle crystalline ore, the facility aims to meet up to 80% of SSP demand, cutting import exposure while addressing sulphur deficiency in soils—SSP supplies phosphorus, sulphur and calcium in one product. In a fertiliser market roiled by LNG-linked urea prices, local phosphates are an orthodox hedge: lower FX leakage, better nutrient balance. 🌱🏭

title: Sri Lanka retools external linkages through skills, defence and trade diplomacy
body: The Ceylon Chamber sent 30 professionals to the Indian Institute of Foreign Trade for a nine‑day immersion spanning trade systems, negotiations, AI and cyber risk, engaging DGFT, FIEO, CII and India’s diplomatic academy. On the security track, Sri Lanka deepened ties with Japan (maritime security, HADR, personnel exchanges) and Indonesia (defence industry, maritime domain awareness), while presenting credentials in Kazakhstan and consulting on a Shanghai Cooperation Organisation development bank. Colombo also deported 125 Chinese nationals over online financial fraud—underscoring rule‑of‑law signalling even as it courts capital. The tilt is pragmatic: skills, standards, and selective enforcement to rebuild trust. 🤝🎓

title: Nature sends signals policymakers can’t ignore
body: Researchers confirmed the presence of the ultra‑rare winghead shark off Bathalangunduwa and warned of juvenile by‑catch in gillnets, hinting at a possible nursery—without management, Sri Lanka could lose a globally unique species. Separately, Parliament’s Environment Oversight Committee ordered an urgent, environmentally sound plan for disposing of Norochcholai’s rising coal ash, and the water utility flagged potential supply restrictions amid drought. With electricity tariffs up and coal quality controversies simmering, the case for faster renewables, storage, and demand management is not ideological—it’s hydrological and ecological. 🦈💧
